work.reflection: I care far more about what a team can build together than about anything I could ship alone - it's a conviction I hold strongly. So even while I was the only engineer here for a while, every decision I made assumed the team we were going to become; as that team grew, the groundwork paid off.
details: Short-term wins drive every piece of work, but I make sure the team's velocity is always spent with the long-term in view - the two are only a trade-off if you let them be.

Through small, persistent steps I delivered internal tooling that hadn't been thought possible - a long-term bet that paid off precisely because I kept carving room for it inside the short-term grind.
We moved onto Railway early, a call I made against the obvious default, and it drove the lesson home: the constraint worth designing around is almost never the technology, it's the team behind it.
I build by directing and interrogating AI rather than writing code by hand - a way of working that only earns its keep because I hold every line to a bar I can fully define and verify.

work.reflection: Xero was my first encounter with genuinely untouched legacy - battle-tested software so load-bearing it had run quietly for years, rightly treated as untouchable. Threading new behaviour through those old foundations without disturbing what already worked forced a patience and precision I'd never had to develop.
details: Working deep in undocumented C# and IIS, reverse-engineering intent straight from the code, is what left me genuinely unafraid to change software I didn't write.
I helped architect the modern .NET replacement while the legacy system stayed live underneath it - holding both in my head at once taught me exactly how much risk hides inside a migration.
Carrying production on-call for systems well beyond my own squad is the fastest way I know to get fluent at reasoning about unfamiliar code while it's still on fire.

work.reflection: University trained me to chase the clever solution; Z Energy retrained me to chase the useful one. Tying what I built to real business outcomes rewired how I think about code - and that shift was harder to accept than any technical problem I faced there.
details: My first role out of university, inside a distributed TypeScript microservices estate run by a team of 10-20 - where the gap between code that works and code a team can live with stopped being theoretical.
Building integrations across third-party vendors and legacy platforms is where messy, real-world constraints stopped reading as noise to me and started reading as the actual engineering problem.

work.reflection: Raygun was my first look at software as it actually lives outside a lecture hall. Helping build a developer-facing library surfaced a whole layer of decisions I had no framework for yet - I couldn't always follow the why, but watching real software breathe under real load was the moment academia stopped being enough.
details: Built and shipped raygun4reactnative with another intern, under senior direction - a published React Native library for crash reporting and real-user monitoring, and my first real taste of work that gets depended on instead of graded.

interests.name: Game Engines
interests.summary: Building my own game engine isn't about reinventing the wheel so much as reinventing the workflow. Mainstream engines are tightly coupled to their own way of working - what lets them scale, but also what asks for more surface knowledge than I've ever been good at carrying. Building it from the ground up, with AI in the loop, is how I go looking for a smaller surface area: one shaped to how I actually think.
interests.cvSummary: Building my own game engine - less to reinvent the wheel than the workflow, trading the surface knowledge mainstream engines demand for a smaller surface area shaped to how I think.

interests.name: Hardware
interests.summary: I've built my own PC, but what stays with me isn't the spec sheet - it's the intuition for what modern hardware can actually do. By that measure something doesn't add up: today's machines are well beyond what last decade's games needed, yet current titles still bring strong hardware to its knees. I read it as an optimisation problem, just not the usual one - the industry pours effort into the low level while the high-level wins go untouched. A lot of careful work, aimed at the wrong layer.
interests.cvSummary: Built my own PC, but the real value is an intuition for what modern hardware can do - and a sense that games today optimise at the wrong layer, chasing low-level wins while the high-level ones go untouched.

interests.name: Ergo Keyboards
interests.summary: This one began as a fix rather than a hobby. A childhood elbow injury left my right wrist with limited rotation - a non-issue at school, until full-time keyboard work turned it into a real one. Solving it pulled me deep into ergonomic boards, through a Planck EZ and a Glove80 and a fair amount of trial and error, and landed me on a ZSA Voyager, which has been my keyboard ever since. Being able to rest my elbow properly again changed things, and I've grown genuinely passionate about the space.
interests.cvSummary: Deep into ergonomic keyboards following a childhood elbow injury - settled on a ZSA Voyager after a long journey through ortholinear and split boards.

interests.name: Linux
interests.summary: I enjoy driving Linux as my daily OS, and when a workflow suits it, that's where I work. I hold it loosely, though - I move between Linux and Windows depending on what the task in front of me actually wants. The control Linux gives is the part I genuinely enjoy; the willingness to switch is just pragmatism.
interests.cvSummary: Happily daily-drive Linux, but move between it and Windows by workflow - the control is the draw, the switching is pragmatism.

interests.name: The Outdoors
interests.summary: Getting outdoors, hiking most often, is where my best ideas tend to surface. Stepping away from the screen gives them room to breathe and connect in ways they never quite do while I'm staring straight at the problem. It reads as time off, but it's closer to a slower way of doing the same work.
interests.cvSummary: Hiking and getting outdoors - where my best ideas tend to surface, with room to breathe away from the screen.
